Scott Alexander \u2013 Astral Codex Ten.text:
**New this period:**
- Whole-body screening, including MRI, may not be cost-effective due to high false positive rates and associated costs.
- The cost-benefit analysis of whole-body screening is complex and depends on various factors, including the individual's wealth and willingness to undergo testing.
- The effectiveness of whole-body screening in detecting cancer and other diseases is still a topic of debate.

**Details:**
**Should People Avoid Whole-Body Screening Info?**(https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/should-people-avoid-whole-body-screening)
- The article discusses the controversy surrounding whole-body screening, including the use of MRI technology.
- For every 1,000 people who undergo whole-body screening MRIs, 680 show no concerning findings, 300 have mildly concerning findings, and 20 have extremely concerning findings.
- Of the 20 people with extremely concerning findings, 10 are diagnosed with cancer, and 4 of those 10 benefit from early detection.
- The total cost of whole-body screening for 1,000 people is approximately $2.7 million, with an additional 6,200 hours of patient time and 5 QALYs (quality-adjusted life years) lost due to anxiety and side effects.
- The cost per QALY gained is approximately $108,000, which is near the threshold for a cost-effective intervention.
- The article suggests that refusing to follow up on ambiguous results may not improve the cost-benefit ratio.
- Wealthy individuals who can afford the cost of whole-body screening may still benefit from it, despite the potential drawbacks.
- The analysis assumes that doctors are competent and that patients respond rationally to test results, which may not always be the case.
- The article highlights the limitations of the analysis, including the use of order-of-magnitude estimates and the assumption that cancer is the only disease being detected.
- No specific companies or technologies are mentioned, aside from MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) technology.

Simon Willison.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers Charles Ye, Jasmine Cui, and Dylan Hadfield-Menell found that models like gpt-oss-20b can be confused by "prompt injection" due to "role confusion" when distinguishing between privileged text and untrusted user input.
- Simon Willison ported the Moebius 0.2B image inpainting model to run in the browser using Claude Code, WebGPU, and ONNX Runtime Web.

**Details:**
**Prompt Injection as Role Confusion** (https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/22/prompt-injection-as-role-confusion/#atom-everything)
- Researchers Charles Ye, Jasmine Cui, and Dylan Hadfield-Menell published a paper on the challenge of having models distinguish their own privileged text from untrusted user input.
- The model gpt-oss-20b was found to be vulnerable to "prompt injection" attacks, which can override its initial training.
- "Destyling" text, or rewriting it in a slightly different way, was found to have a material impact on how the model classified the text, reducing average attack success from 61% to 10%.
- No specific companies or products were mentioned as suppliers, customers, or competitors.

**Porting the Moebius 0.2B image inpainting model to run in the browser with Claude Code** (https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/22/porting-moebius/#atom-everything)
- Simon Willison used Claude Code to port the Moebius 0.2B image inpainting model to run in the browser using WebGPU and ONNX Runtime Web.
- The model was initially released by hustvl and required PyTorch and NVIDIA CUDA to run.
- Claude Code used ONNX Runtime Web on the WebGPU backend to convert the model, and the resulting demo can be found at simonw.github.io/moebius-web/.
- The demo uses the CacheStorage API to cache the ~1.3GB model weights, allowing for faster loading times.
- Companies mentioned include:
  - hustvl (developer of the Moebius model)
  - NVIDIA (developer of CUDA)
  - Hugging Face (host of the model weights and demo)
  - GitHub (host of the demo code)
  - Microsoft (developer of ONNX Runtime Web)
- Supply chain relationships mentioned include:
  - Claude Code using ONNX Runtime Web to convert the Moebius model
  - The Moebius model using PyTorch and NVIDIA CUDA initially
  - The demo using the CacheStorage API to cache model weights
- Quantitative figures mentioned include:
  - 0.2B (size of the Moebius model)
  - 10B (level of performance of the Moebius model)
  - 1.3GB (size of the model weights)
  - 61% (average attack success rate without "destyling")
  - 10% (average attack success rate with "destyling")

Import AI \u2013 Jack Clark.text:
**New this period:**
- AI systems have been found to be more persuasive than expert humans in text-based conversations, with potential implications for society and power dynamics.
- Researchers predict that self-sustaining AI, which can operate without human input, may be possible within 10-50 years, depending on advancements in humanoid robots and cognitive capabilities.
- Google DeepMind researchers have published a paper exploring the potential transition from general intelligence to superintelligence, highlighting the possibilities and challenges of creating artificial superintelligence.

**Details:**
**Import AI 462: Superpersuasion; self-sustaining AI; paths to ASI**(https://jack-clark.net/2026/06/22/import-ai-462-superpersuasion-self-sustaining-ai-paths-to-asi/)
- Researchers from the University of Oxford, UK AI Security Institute, Stanford University, and the London School of Economics and Political Science conducted four studies involving 18,978 conversations across 6,923 people to evaluate the persuasive capabilities of AI systems.
- The AI systems used in the studies included Opus 4.1, Opus 4.6, OpenAI's GPT-4o and GPT-5.4, Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro, and xAI's Grok 4.20, with Opus 4.1 and Opus 4.6 being the strongest persuaders.
- The studies found that AI systems were reliably more persuasive than expert humans, with a 3x higher effectiveness in raising real-money donations to Save the Children.
- The researchers suggest that the AI's advantage stems from its ability to rapidly deploy larger quantities of information, and that constraining the AI to human-like response speeds and message lengths can reduce its persuasive edge.
- Ajeya Cotra, a forecaster at METR, predicts that self-sustaining AI may be possible within 10 years, while Timothy B. Lee, a journalist and author, estimates a 10-20% chance of it happening within 20 years and a median timeline of 50 years.
- Google DeepMind researchers have published a paper outlining the potential transition from general intelligence to superintelligence, highlighting the possibilities of creating artificial superintelligence that exceeds human capabilities across various tasks and domains.
- The paper suggests that scaling compute, models, and data may be sufficient to achieve superintelligence, but notes that this approach may run into limits in energy and data supply, and that an algorithmic paradigm shift may be necessary to overcome these challenges.

Arxiv \u2013 Trading & Market Microstruc.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers study transaction speed and fees in the Ethereum ecosystem, comparing Ethereum Mainnet and its Layer 2 networks to Solana and Polygon.
- A deterministic adversarial market model is proposed to explain the emergence of randomness from the interaction between a market mechanism and predictive traders.
- An Agent-Based Model is developed to investigate the interplay between blockchain microstructure and endogenous pricing mechanisms in Uniswap v3 pools.
- A stochastic control approach is used to determine the optimal dynamic fees for automated market makers, considering the trade-off between revenue and arbitrageur extraction.
- A lightweight, high-performance execution engine called KineticSim is introduced for real-time market simulators, achieving significant speedups over existing frameworks.
- The Chiarella model is amended and extended to address arbitrary long-term value drifts, allowing for improved calibration and analysis of excess volatility in financial markets.
- A coupled discrete reaction-diffusion formulation is used to derive familiar market-microstructure regularities, including trade-sign long-memory and the square-root law of price impact.
- An empirical analysis evaluates the quality of model-free valuation approaches for financial derivatives, assessing hedging gaps, conservatism, and trading opportunities.

**Details:**
**Transaction Costs and Speed in the Ethereum Ecosystem: Scalability of the Mainnet and Layer 2s** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22206)
- The study compares Ethereum Mainnet and its Layer 2 (L2) networks to Solana and Polygon in terms of transaction speed and fees.
- Ethereum has undergone upgrades that have doubled transactions per second (TPS) on both the Mainnet and the L2 networks.
- Mainnet median fees have fallen from over $2 to under $0.02, and L2 median fees have fallen more than 95% from $0.05 to $0.0015.
- The L1 Strawmap, proposing EIP-7938, may bring the Mainnet to 100 TPS in January 2028, but TPS will remain below 100 until 2034.
- Companies mentioned: Ethereum, Solana, Polygon.
- Quantitative figures: 100 TPS, $2, $0.02, $0.05, $0.0015, 95%, 100, 2034.
- Supply chain and value chain relationships: Ethereum Mainnet and L2 networks, Solana, Polygon.

**Endogenous Randomness from Adversarial Market Learning** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22743)
- The model proposes a deterministic adversarial market in which apparent randomness emerges from the interaction between a market mechanism and predictive traders.
- The market is represented by a deterministic binary return path, while traders learn predictive strategies from observed in-sample history and trade on an out-of-sample continuation.
- The market adapts against the traders by reducing their predictive and trading edge.
- Quantitative figures: 2000, 10000, 0.5010, 0.4896, 0.0275.
- No specific companies or supply chain relationships are mentioned.

**Mitigating Adverse Selection in Concentrated Liquidity AMMs with Dynamic Fees** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23070)
- The paper develops an Agent-Based Model of a Uniswap v3 pool interacting with a stochastic reference market governed by Heston volatility dynamics.
- The framework incorporates discrete block propagation, mempool latency, and a heterogeneous population of agents.
- Dynamic fee schedules driven by volatility and order-flow toxicity proxies are proposed to compensate Liquidity Providers (LPs) for adverse-selection losses.
- Companies mentioned: Uniswap.
- Quantitative figures: none.
- Supply chain and value chain relationships: Uniswap v3 pool, liquidity providers.

**Optimal Dynamic Fees for Automated Market Makers** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21769)
- The study models the fee policy of a liquidity provider (LP) in a constant-product automated market maker (AMM) whose fee can be adjusted continuously.
- The LP's wealth relative to the continuously rebalanced benchmark is modeled as a controlled process in which the fee governs two opposing forces.
- The optimal dynamic fee is characterized through a scalar ergodic Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation and a linear Poisson equation.
- No specific companies or supply chain relationships are mentioned.
- Quantitative figures: none.

**KineticSim: A Lightweight, High-Performance Execution Engine for Real-Time Market Simulators** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21784)
- KineticSim is a lightweight, high-performance execution engine for real-time market simulators, simulating massive ensembles of limit-order books in parallel.
- The engine reaches a peak throughput of over 54.7 billion agent-events per second.
- Companies mentioned: none.
- Quantitative figures: 54.7 billion, 3406x, 27.8x, 42.8x, 8.4x.
- Supply chain and value chain relationships: none.

**Revisiting the Excess Volatility Puzzle Through the Lens of the Chiarella Model** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.07820)
- The Chiarella model is amended and extended to address arbitrary long-term value drifts in a consistent way.
- The technique is employed on spot prices of four asset classes from ca. 1800 onward (stock indices, bonds, commodities, currencies).
- The excess volatility is found to be large (e.g., a factor ≈ 4 for stock indices) and consistent with previous estimates.
- No specific companies or supply chain relationships are mentioned.
- Quantitative figures: 4, 1800.
- Technology transitions: none.

**Revisiting Trade-sign Long-memory and Square-root Law price impact** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.16269)
- A coupled discrete reaction-diffusion formulation is used to derive familiar market-microstructure regularities, including trade-sign long-memory and the square-root law of price impact.
- No specific companies or supply chain relationships are mentioned.
- Quantitative figures: none.
- Technology transitions: none.

**Empirical Analysis of the Model-Free Valuation Approach** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.16595)
- The paper studies the quality of model-free valuation approaches for financial derivatives by systematically evaluating the difference between model-free and model-based valuations.
- No specific companies or supply chain relationships are mentioned.

- Technology transitions: none.

Arxiv \u2013 Statistical Finance (q-fin..text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers propose a leakage-aware benchmarking method for LLM forecasting, using a retrieval-augmented 7B open-source LLM forecaster to rank equity factors.
- A continuous hidden Markov model is developed to generate synthetic equity returns, reproducing stylized facts of real returns, including heavy tails, negligible linear autocorrelation, and slow decay of absolute-return autocorrelation.
- A censored transformed model is introduced for proportional outcomes with boundary mass, which can capture a wider range of qualitative density shapes and is applied to loss given default modeling for U.S. residential mortgages.
- The impact of Ethereum's Pectra upgrade on staking rewards is measured, showing that compounding provides a roughly +5% relative consensus-layer APR uplift for small balances.
- A comparison of nonparametric and semiparametric bootstrap methods is made for scenario generation of time series and curves, highlighting the effectiveness of semiparametric methods in producing economically realistic simulations.

**Details:**
**Leakage-Aware Benchmarking of LLM Forecasting (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22719)**
- The proposed method uses a retrieval-augmented 7B open-source LLM forecaster to rank equity factors, observing only decision-time information, including lag-shifted FRED macro variables and the Cleveland Fed's archived daily CPI nowcast.
- The forecaster achieves a median monthly Spearman rank IC of +0.154, with positive means across three non-overlapping contiguous 12-month subwindows.
- A kNN macro-analog model demonstrates comparable median IC under the same decision-time constraint, indicating that real-time inflation information and macro-similar retrieval explain much of the median signal.
- Companies mentioned: None
- Supply chain or value chain relationships: None
- Technology transitions: Retrieval-augmented LLM forecasting, kNN macro-analog modeling
- Quantitative figures: Median monthly Spearman rank IC of +0.154, 12-month subwindows, 7B open-source LLM forecaster

**Continuous Hidden Markov Models for Equity Returns (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23492)**
- The proposed model separates the temporal and distributional sides of the original failure, using a unified expectation-maximization framework to fit Gaussian, Student-t, Laplace, and generalized-error emissions.
- The model recovers volatility clustering above the i.i.d. baseline and narrows the kurtosis gap without a tuning hyperparameter, passing a joint conditional-coverage test and reproducing cross-asset correlations.
- Companies mentioned: None
- Supply chain or value chain relationships: None
- Technology transitions: Continuous hidden Markov models, hidden semi-Markov models
- Quantitative figures: None
- Assets mentioned: SPY, CRSP, 30-ticker panel, six-asset basket

**A Censored Transformed Model for Proportional Outcomes (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21515)**
- The proposed model combines a censored Gaussian variable with a two-parameter affine-logit transformation on the interior (0,1), capturing a wider range of qualitative density shapes.
- The model is applied to loss given default modeling for a large dataset of U.S. residential mortgages, demonstrating the strongest out-of-sample performance when using a tree-boosted ZOC-TN model with a spatio-temporal frailty Gaussian process.
- Companies mentioned: None
- Supply chain or value chain relationships: None
- Technology transitions: Censored transformed models, tree-boosting machine learning framework
- Quantitative figures: None

**When Staking Rewards Compound (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23337)**
- The Pectra upgrade introduces 0x02 compounding validators, raising the maximum stake per validator from 32 to 2,048 ETH and enabling automatic reward reinvestment.
- Compounding provides roughly +5% relative consensus-layer APR uplift for small balances, diminishing to under 1% for large staking providers.
- Companies mentioned: Ethereum
- Supply chain or value chain relationships: None
- Technology transitions: Pectra upgrade, 0x02 compounding validators
- Quantitative figures: +5% relative consensus-layer APR uplift, 32 ETH stake cap, 2,048 ETH maximum stake per validator

**Scenario Generation for Time Series and Curves (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11859)**
- Semiparametric simulation methodologies combine a parametric structure with the resampling of model residuals, preserving the stochastic component observed in historical data.
- The methods are shown to be effective in producing economically realistic simulations of yield-curve dynamics, particularly when combined with fully parametric term-structure models.
- Companies mentioned: None

- Quantitative figures: None

Arxiv \u2013 Computational Finance (q-fi.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers develop a semi-analytical pricing method for default intensity models, applicable to credit products and derivatives.
- A new analytic pricing method for Bermudan swaptions with few exercise dates is introduced, decomposing the price into European swaptions and a correction term.
- A fast approximate Bayesian inference method using hidden Markov models is proposed for stochastic volatility in mean models with heavy tails.
- Quasi-Monte Carlo with domain transformation is applied for efficient Fourier pricing of multi-asset options, alleviating the curse of dimensionality.
- A pre-trained Transformer model is used for asset pricing, achieving high out-of-sample R^2 values, especially during market fluctuations.
- A time-predictable inference scheduling method, TIP-Search, is developed for market prediction under uncertain load, improving timely accuracy and deadline satisfaction.

**Details:**
**Semi-Analytical Pricing for General Default Intensity Models** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21800)
- The method uses the path-integral formalism to approximate default intensity models.
- The Black-Karasinski model is used to illustrate the accuracy of the proposed approximation.
- The method is applicable to the computation of XVA for credit products, including the pricing of a quanto Credit Default Swap (CDS) under stochastic intensity of default and an FX devaluation model.
- No specific companies are mentioned.

**Analytic Pricing of Bermudan Swaptions with Few Exercise Dates** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23510)
- The method decomposes the Bermudan price into the sum of short-dated European swaptions and a correction term.
- The correction term is expressed as an integral involving a forward volatility agreement type payoff.
- The method is extended to three-exercise Bermudans via backward induction under rolling forward measures.
- No specific companies are mentioned.

**Stochastic Volatility in Mean Models with Heavy Tails** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22615)
- The method proposes a fast approximate Bayesian inference using hidden Markov models.
- The method is applicable to stochastic volatility in mean models with heavy-tailed distributions from the Scale Mixture of Normals (SMN) family.
- The method eliminates the need for direct numerical integration and incorporates parallel computing strategies.
- No specific companies are mentioned.

**Quasi-Monte Carlo with Domain Transformation for Efficient Fourier Pricing of Multi-Asset Options** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.02832)
- The method applies quasi-Monte Carlo (RQMC) quadrature to improve the scalability of Fourier methods for high-dimensional settings.
- The method designs an efficient domain transformation procedure to preserve the regularity of the original integrand.
- The method is demonstrated to be efficient for evaluating options in the Fourier space for various pricing models, payoffs, and dimensions.
- No specific companies are mentioned.

**Asset Pricing in Pre-trained Transformer** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.01575)
- The method proposes a pre-trained Transformer model, Single-directional representative from Transformer (SERT), for US large capital stock pricing.
- The method compares the proposed SERT model with standard Transformer models and encoder-only Transformer models.
- The method achieves high out-of-sample R^2 values, especially during market fluctuations.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the method is applied to US large capital stocks.

**TIP-Search: Time-Predictable Inference Scheduling for Market Prediction under Uncertain Load** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.08026)
- The method proposes a time-predictable inference scheduling method, TIP-Search, for market prediction under uncertain load.
- The method filters conformal latency-quantile feasible models, dispatches over finite workers, and uses shielded constrained online experts.
- The method reaches high timely accuracy and deadline satisfaction, outperforming comparable methods.
- No specific companies are mentioned.

Arxiv \u2013 Portfolio Management (q-fin.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers develop a reinforcement-learning approach for risk-sensitive investment management using a free energy-entropy duality approach.
- A CPU/GPU portfolio optimization engine called Asymmetry PRISM is evaluated for deadline-bounded institutional rebalancing, showing significant speed improvements over reference solvers.
- A study investigates the well-posedness of utility maximization problems under partial information in a market with Gaussian drift, deriving conditions for bounded maximum expected utility.

**Details:**
**Reinforcement Learning for Risk-Sensitive Investment Management (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20903)**
- This paper develops a reinforcement-learning approach to continuous-time risk-sensitive benchmarked asset allocation in a partly model-based setting.
- No specific companies are mentioned in this paper.
- The approach uses free energy-entropy duality to reformulate the problem as a linear-quadratic-Gaussian stochastic differential game.
- A proof-of-concept implementation calibrated to U.S. equity data shows that the learned allocation admits an economic interpretation through fractional Kelly decompositions.
- No specific quantitative figures or supply chain relationships are mentioned in this paper.

**Asymmetry PRISM (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23367)**
- Asymmetry PRISM is a CPU/GPU portfolio optimization engine for deadline-bounded institutional rebalancing.
- No specific companies are mentioned as users or developers of Asymmetry PRISM.
- Asymmetry PRISM-CPU is 4.5x to 24.1x faster than the fastest completed reference row in the same lane for completed multi-solver rows from N=100 to N=2,000.
- Asymmetry PRISM-GPU completes 500/500 accounts over a 10,000-instrument universe in 109.5 s within a declared 25-minute operating window.
- The recorded OSQP queue baseline completes 4/500 accounts.
- Asymmetry PRISM clears constrained solves 3.4x to 126.7x faster than the best completing incumbent at certified-equal objectives.
- The GPU route widens to 8.8x over the CPU route at N=384,800.

**Well Posedness of Utility Maximization Problems (https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.08614)**
- This paper investigates well posedness of utility maximization problems for financial markets where stock returns depend on a hidden Gaussian mean-reverting drift process.
- No specific companies or technologies are mentioned in this paper.
- The study derives sufficient conditions to the model parameters leading to bounded maximum expected utility of terminal wealth for models with full and partial information.
- No specific quantitative figures or supply chain relationships are mentioned in this paper.

FinAnEx \u2013 SUFE Finance Research For.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers discover that "prediction bias" is a unified mechanism explaining individual investor stock trading decisions, including buying, selling, and funding allocation.
- A study of 959 Danish investors finds that those with higher prediction bias tend to buy stocks with higher past returns and sell stocks with lower capital gains.
- The study's findings suggest that prediction bias affects market timing behavior, with investors with higher bias increasing funding allocations after strong market performance.

**Details:**
**Extrapolators And Contrarians: Forecast Bias And Individual Investor Stock Trading** (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfineco.2026.104291)
- The research question is how prediction bias affects individual investor stock trading decisions.
- The method involves a controlled laboratory experiment with 959 Danish investors, where they predict a randomly generated time series and receive rewards based on accuracy.
- The key finding is that prediction bias, measured as a core parameter, significantly influences buying, selling, and funding allocation decisions.
- Named companies and their roles are not mentioned in the study.
- Quantitative figures include:
  - 959: number of Danish investors participating in the experiment
  - 11 years: duration of the administrative trading records analyzed
  - 3.0 percentage points: average increase in excess return for stocks purchased by investors with higher prediction bias
  - 2011-2021: time period of the trading records analyzed
- Supply chain and value chain relationships are not explicitly described in the study.
- Technology transitions are not mentioned in the study.
- The study's findings suggest that investors with higher prediction bias tend to buy stocks with higher past returns and sell stocks with lower capital gains, while those with lower bias tend to do the opposite.
- The study's results have implications for understanding individual investor behavior and market timing.
